food_subgroup: Mixed seafood set meal with rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a Malaysian prawn and squid set meal. FOODON_03315595 (seafood dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Crustaceans and molluscs, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for a combined prawn-squid Malaysian set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Malaysian seafood set meal combination.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal plate consisting of approximately 100 g steamed white rice, 80–100 g cooked prawns, 80–100 g cooked squid, and a portion of sambal or curry sauce, totalling approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: IMR Malaysia MyFCD — Cooked Prawns and Squid with Rice; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables for Southeast Asia; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Shrimp cooked and Squid cooked
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) / FAO INFOODS / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a combined prawn-and-squid set meal as served in Malaysian-Singaporean restaurants. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition: approximately 29% steamed white rice (~100 g), 29% cooked prawns (~100 g), 29% cooked squid (~100 g), and 13% sambal/sauce (~45 g). Per-100g values are derived from IMR MyFCD entries for cooked prawns and squid, FAO/INFOODS SEA rice values, and USDA SR Legacy for cross-validation. The composite reflects a typical restaurant serving of this dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice and Mixed Seafood Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a combined prawn-sotong set meal. GI is estimated as a weighted composite: steamed white rice (GI ~72, high, University of Sydney GI Database) contributes approximately 29% of the meal weight and dominates glycemic response; cooked prawns and squid contribute negligible carbohydrate and effectively lower the meal GI. The composite meal GI is estimated at approximately 54 (low), consistent with published mixed seafood-rice meal GI values in Atkinson et al. 2021. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 44.8) / 100 ≈ 24.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 12.8 × 3.5 = 44.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from sambal sauce and cooking process
hypertension.guidance: A single set meal serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium (310 mg per 100 g × 3.5), which represents nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce portions or choose steamed preparations to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving is in the moderate range, primarily due to the white rice component; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting brown rice where available, and pairing with the high-protein prawn and squid components to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content from prawns and squid, and sodium from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns and squid are naturally higher in dietary cholesterol (approximately 115 mg per 100 g of the composite dish); while dietary cholesterol has a modest effect on LDL in most individuals, the combined sodium load and cooking oil used in sambal preparation warrant portion moderation for those with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn and Sotong Set Meal is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean hawker and casual restaurant dish consisting of steamed white rice served alongside cooked prawns (udang) and squid (sotong), typically prepared with sambal, curry sauce, or stir-fried with garlic, chilli, and onion. The dish is a staple offering at Malaysian banana leaf and seafood restaurants in Singapore, reflecting the multicultural Malay-Chinese-Indian culinary heritage of the region. Prawns contribute a firm, sweet, and slightly briny flavour, while squid offers a tender-chewy texture with a mild oceanic taste. The sambal or curry sauce adds heat, umami depth, and aromatic complexity from lemongrass, galangal, and dried chilli. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal providing approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, with a good protein content of around 10 g per 100 g from the seafood components. The dish is a meaningful source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine from the prawns and squid. Sodium content is elevated due to the sauce preparation and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic response is driven primarily by the white rice component, yielding a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 54 and a glycemic load of approximately 24 per standard serving. The dish is naturally dairy-free and halal-suitable. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner set meal at prices around SGD 5 in Singapore hawker and casual dining contexts.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a combined prawn-and-squid set meal as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, and USDA SR Legacy using a weighted composition model reflecting a typical restaurant serving. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and published mixed seafood-rice meal data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from approved sources is strong and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source databases for this food category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary approved sources for this composite dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in cooked seafood and rice per USDA SR Legacy and IMR MyFCD — confirmed zero for this food type. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy seafood entries and are approximate. The food_name has been normalised from 'Prawn / Sotong Set Meal' to 'Prawn and Sotong Set Meal' for canonical English identity. The restaurant source (Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant - Circular Road) is noted for provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source. Price data (SGD 5.00) is preserved from the input record for contextual reference.
